docs.title: Building Autonomous Robots From Scratch
docs.text:
Learn robotics the way humanity could have invented it.

Not by memorizing formulas.

Not by copying tutorials.

By rebuilding every invention from first principles.

Every discovery begins with a question.

Every discovery ends with something that moves.

docs.title: Start
docs.text: • Read: Discovery 01 — Why Wheels Move

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text:
You have invented the wheel.

It still doesn't move.

Why?

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text:
You have: - One wheel - A flat floor

How can motion begin?

docs.title: Exercise
docs.text: Sketch three different ways to make the wheel move before reading further.

docs.title: Engineering Notes
docs.text:
The wheel alone is not enough. Motion always requires an interaction:

  • Force
  • Friction
  • Constraint

These ideas will appear throughout robotics.

Continue → Why Things Refuse to Move

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text: You push a heavy crate. It does not budge. Why does the world resist changes in motion?

docs.title: Thinking
docs.text:
Before we name anything, ask yourself:

  • What would happen if the missing piece were absent?
  • What is the simplest system that could show this effect?
  • Can you draw the interaction before reading the answer?

docs.title: Discovery
docs.text: Objects resist changes in velocity. This property is called inertia.

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
We build a minimal inertia model in Python.

Run the implementation:

python python/chapter02/main.py

docs.title: Simulation
docs.text:
Run the chapter simulation:

python simulations/chapter02/sim.py

A browser version is available at browser/chapter02/index.html.

docs.title: Exercises
docs.text:
1. Change one parameter in the simulation and predict what will happen.
  2. Draw the system before and after the discovery.
  3. Name one real-world device that depends on this idea and one way it can fail.

docs.title: Engineering Notes
docs.text:
Real systems add noise, latency, and power limits. The model we built is the simplest version; real inertia designs trade accuracy, cost, and reliability.

Continue → Why Magnets Pull

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text: A magnet and a nail attract each other through empty space. What is happening between them?

Continue → Why Motors Need Conductors

  • What is the simplest system that could show this effect?

docs.title: Discovery

Continue → Why Feedback Changes Everything

Continue → Why Guessing Isn't Control

Before we name anything, ask yourself:

  • Can you draw the interaction before reading the answer?

Continue → Why Errors Accumulate

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text: Small mistakes in direction add up over a long walk. How do tiny errors become huge errors?

docs.title: Thinking
docs.text:
Before we name anything, ask yourself:

  • What would happen if the missing piece were absent?
  • What is the simplest system that could show this effect?
  • Can you draw the interaction before reading the answer?

docs.title: Discovery
docs.text: Errors integrate over time, so a controller must remove steady bias.

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:

Continue → Why PID Was Invented

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text: Proportional control leaves offset; integral control is slow; derivative control is noisy. How do we combine them?

docs.title: Thinking
docs.text:
Before we name anything, ask yourself:

  • What would happen if the missing piece were absent?
  • What is the simplest system that could show this effect?
  • Can you draw the interaction before reading the answer?

docs.title: Discovery

Continue → Why Sensors Disagree

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text: Two sensors report different positions. Which one is correct?

docs.title: Thinking
docs.text:
Before we name anything, ask yourself:

  • What would happen if the missing piece were absent?
  • What is the simplest system that could show this effect?
  • Can you draw the interaction before reading the answer?

docs.title: Discovery
docs.text: Every sensor has noise, bias, and a different frame of reference.

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
We build a minimal sensor disagreement model in Python.

Run the implementation:

python python/chapter23/main.py

Continue → the next discovery

docs.title: Philosophy
docs.text:
• Discover before defining.
  • Build before abstracting.
  • No black boxes.
  • Every chapter ends with code.
